metadata.document: 2026 AHA/ASA Guideline for Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
edition_notes: v3 active: injected into both LLM prompts via query_parsing_agent._build_topic_map_appendix. Verifier imports the same builder so parser and verifier see identical topic descriptions.

v3 addition: every subtopic now carries an addresses field (23 subtopics annotated) so the LLM can disambiguate between sibling subsections like 4.6.2 vs 4.6.3 vs 4.6.4 vs 4.6.5 using full prose context, not just the qualifier label.
v3 namespace: sole live copy lives at agents/qa_v3/references/.
metadata.purpose: Maps clinical topics to guideline sections. The LLM classifies the user's question into a topic from this list. Python looks up the section. The topic IS the routing — no keyword matching.

usage.llm: Reads the topic list + 'addresses' descriptions to classify the question. Picks one topic. Optionally picks a subtopic qualifier.
usage.python: Looks up topic → section. If qualifier provided, narrows to subtopic section. Direct lookup, no scoring.

topics.addresses: Public education programs for stroke recognition in all ages. Covers FAST/BE-FAST campaigns, community awareness, reaching diverse populations, reducing time from symptom onset to 911 call. Also covers education for EMS professionals and physicians. This is about population-level education, NOT about clinical assessment of individual patients.

Regional systems of stroke care from the EMS perspective: prehospital triage protocols, EMS quality metrics, dispatcher recognition protocols, reducing delays from symptom onset to treatment. This is about how EMS systems are organized, NOT about what EMS does for an individual patient (see Prehospital Assessment for that).

What EMS does for individual patients in the ambulance: prehospital stroke screening scales (LAMS, RACE, CPSS, FAST-ED, VAN), field assessment for LVO, advance hospital notification, prehospital blood pressure management, IV access. Also covers prehospital treatments that were studied and found NOT beneficial: remote ischemic conditioning (RIC, arm BP cuff inflation), transdermal glyceryl trinitrate (GTN/nitroglycerin) for BP lowering, intensive field BP control to 130-140 mmHg. 7 recommendations.

Which hospital to transport the patient to: bypass decisions (skip closer hospital for stroke center), drip-and-ship (start IVT then transfer) vs mothership (go directly to CSC/TSC), PSC vs CSC vs TSC selection, interhospital transfer protocols and agreements, DIDO (door-in-door-out) time targets. 5 recommendations.

Mobile stroke unit (MSU) vehicles with onboard CT scanner and telemedicine. Covers: MSU vs conventional EMS outcomes, MSU equipment requirements (must be able to diagnose and treat with IVT), MSU role in identifying and triaging EVT-eligible patients to appropriate hospitals, MSU impact on treatment times (DTN, DIDO). MSU is a single compound concept — a specialized ambulance, not 'mobile' + 'stroke' + 'unit' separately.

Stroke center certification and what each level provides: acute stroke-ready hospital (ASRH), primary stroke center (PSC), thrombectomy-capable stroke center (TSC), comprehensive stroke center (CSC). Covers certification by external credentialing bodies, including pediatric stroke capabilities. 1 recommendation about certification benefit.

Organized ED protocols for rapid stroke evaluation: code stroke activation, stroke team designation (clinicians + nurses + lab), door-to-imaging targets, onset-to-treatment time goals for IVT and EVT. Covers both adult and pediatric patients (including first-time seizure as possible stroke). 5 recommendations about team structure and time targets.

Telestroke and teleradiology systems for hospitals without on-site stroke expertise: prehospital telemedicine in ambulances, remote imaging interpretation (teleradiology), telestroke for IVT decision-making at hospitals without neurologists, telestroke effectiveness vs usual care for mortality and functional outcomes. 7 recommendations. Route here for questions about remote stroke consultation, NOT for general telemedicine questions outside stroke.

How stroke centers work together as a network: participation in organized stroke systems of care (SSOC), interhospital transfer agreements and protocols, comprehensive monitoring of EVT patients transferred from other hospitals, credentialing of neurointerventionalists, hub-and-spoke coordination, regionalization. 8 recommendations about hospital-to-hospital coordination.

Quality improvement programs and data collection: GWTG-Stroke and other stroke registries, multicomponent QI processes, adherence monitoring, baseline NIHSS documentation for all stroke patients. 3 recommendations.

Clinical stroke severity measurement: NIHSS as the recommended severity scale, use in treatment decisions (IVT/EVT eligibility), prognostication, serial monitoring. 1 recommendation. This covers WHAT scale to use, not HOW to score it. For questions about specific NIHSS score thresholds for treatment, route to IVT (4.6) or EVT (4.7) instead.

All neuroimaging for acute stroke evaluation: NCCT (noncontrast CT), CTA (CT angiography), CTP (CT perfusion), MRI/MRA/DWI/FLAIR, ASPECTS scoring, penumbra/core mismatch imaging, Tmax >6s definition, DWI-FLAIR mismatch for unknown-onset stroke, LVO detection, imaging for wake-up stroke, DTAS (direct triage to angiography suite), pediatric imaging protocols. 11 recommendations. This is the largest imaging section — covers initial brain imaging, vascular imaging, and advanced perfusion imaging. Route here for any question about WHAT imaging to get, WHEN to image, or HOW to interpret imaging for stroke. Do NOT route imaging questions to specific treatment sections (4.6, 4.7) unless the question is specifically about treatment eligibility criteria.

Non-imaging diagnostic workup for suspected stroke: ECG (electrocardiogram, EKG) — recommended but should not delay IVT/EVT; troponin — recommended but should not delay IVT/EVT; blood glucose — must check before IVT; platelet count, coagulation studies (INR, aPTT), renal function. 2 recommendations. Route here for questions about lab tests, blood work, cardiac monitoring, ECG, or troponin in acute stroke. NOT for imaging (see Imaging topic).

Airway management and oxygen therapy in acute stroke: intubation for decreased consciousness or bulbar dysfunction; supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >94% in hypoxic patients; supplemental oxygen NOT recommended in non-hypoxic patients ineligible for EVT; hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) for arterial air embolism causing stroke (may be reasonable, COR 2b); HBO NOT recommended for AIS not caused by air embolism. Also covers NBO (normobaric hyperoxia) for EVT candidates. 6 recommendations. IMPORTANT: HBO lives here as a treatment for air embolism, NOT under Neuroprotection (4.11). Route HBO questions here.

Head-of-bed (HOB) elevation in acute stroke: flat (0 degrees) vs elevated (30 degrees) positioning for 24 hours. No benefit of routine flat positioning overall. For large artery atherosclerosis without reperfusion options, flat positioning may have uncertain benefit. 2 recommendations. Very narrow topic — only route here for specific questions about head position, HOB angle, or lying flat.

Blood pressure targets and management in acute stroke across all scenarios: (1) Hypotension/hypovolemia correction to maintain perfusion; (2) Urgent BP treatment only for comorbid conditions (acute coronary event, heart failure, aortic dissection, preeclampsia); (3) BP >=220/120 without IVT/EVT — lowering by 15% in first 24h is reasonable; (4) BP <220/120 without IVT/EVT — starting/restarting BP meds not beneficial in first 48-72h; (5) Pre-IVT target: SBP <185, DBP <110; (6) Pre-EVT without IVT: maintain <=185/110; (7) Post-IVT: maintain <180/105 for 24h; (8) Post-EVT with successful reperfusion: SBP <140 may be reasonable; (9) Post-EVT without successful reperfusion: SBP <180 is reasonable; (10) Avoid SBP <140 in non-reperfused patients. 10 recommendations with specific numeric thresholds. This is the primary section for ALL BP questions in AIS.

Fever (hyperthermia) management in acute stroke: targeting normothermia with nurse-initiated fever protocols is recommended; identify and treat sources of fever (infection); induced hypothermia and prophylactic fever prevention are NOT recommended in normothermic patients. 3 recommendations. Route here for questions about fever, temperature targets, cooling, or hypothermia after stroke.

Blood sugar management in acute stroke: treat hypoglycemia (<60 mg/dL) to avoid complications; target persistent hyperglycemia to 140-180 mg/dL range (reasonable, COR 2a); intensive IV insulin targeting 80-130 mg/dL is NOT recommended (COR 3: No Benefit). 3 recommendations with specific glucose thresholds. Route here for questions about blood sugar, glucose, insulin, hyperglycemia, or hypoglycemia in stroke.

Intravenous thrombolysis — the parent section covering all IVT subtopics. If the question is about IVT generally, start here. For specific IVT subtopics, use qualifiers: choice of drug (alteplase vs tenecteplase) → 4.6.2; extended time windows (4.5-24h, wake-up stroke, perfusion mismatch) → 4.6.3; other thrombolytics (reteplase, streptokinase, desmoteplase, prourokinase, sonothrombolysis) → 4.6.4; special circumstances (sickle cell disease, CRAO/retinal artery occlusion) → 4.6.5. For IVT eligibility/contraindications, see IVT Indications and Contraindications topic.

subtopics.addresses: Choice of Thrombolytic Agent (§4.6.2). Which fibrinolytic to administer when IVT is already indicated: alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) as standard, tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg) as non-inferior alternative supported by AcT, ATTEST-2, TASTE, NOR-TEST, EXTEND-IA TNK trials. Covers head-to-head comparison, dosing, and when tenecteplase is preferred. Does NOT cover eligibility, contraindications, or the decision of WHETHER to give IVT (see IVT Indications and Contraindications §4.6.1). Does NOT cover extended time window selection (see §4.6.3). Does NOT cover streptokinase, desmoteplase, prourokinase, reteplase (see §4.6.4).

Extended Time Windows for Intravenous Thrombolysis (§4.6.3). IVT administered beyond the standard 4.5-hour window. Covers wake-up stroke and unknown-onset stroke selected by DWI-FLAIR mismatch (WAKE-UP trial), perfusion imaging mismatch selection (EXTEND, EPITHET, ECASS-4 trials, 4.5-9h window), and the 4.5-9h IV alteplase eligibility criteria. Tenecteplase in extended window (TIMELESS, ROSE-TNK). Does NOT cover standard 0-4.5h IVT (that is baseline eligibility — see §4.6.1 / Table 4). Does NOT cover EVT extended window 6-24h (see §4.7.2 — different trials: DAWN, DEFUSE-3). Route 'DWI-FLAIR mismatch', 'wake-up stroke IVT', 'IVT beyond 4.5 hours' questions here.

Other IV Fibrinolytics and Sonothrombolysis (§4.6.4). Non-alteplase / non-tenecteplase IV thrombolytic options: reteplase (RAISE trial — superior to alteplase at 0.18+0.18 U/kg in China), desmoteplase (DIAS trials — no benefit), streptokinase (MOST trial — increased mortality, not recommended), and adjunctive sonothrombolysis (NOR-SASS, CLOTBUST-ER — no clinical benefit). Does NOT cover alteplase vs tenecteplase comparison (see §4.6.2). Does NOT cover intra-arterial thrombolysis (legacy, not a current recommendation).

Other Specific Circumstances (§4.6.5). IVT in patient populations with specific considerations: sickle cell disease (IVT reasonable), central/branch retinal artery occlusion (CRAO/BRAO — IVT within 4.5h is reasonable), and pediatric patients (<18y). Covers whether IVT should be given when standard eligibility criteria intersect with an unusual patient factor. Does NOT cover the full Table 8 contraindication list (see §4.6.1 / Table 8). Does NOT cover pregnancy, recent surgery, DOAC/warfarin use as standalone topics — those are contraindication entries in Table 8; the qualifier label above is aspirational and may be loose.

topics.addresses: Endovascular thrombectomy — the parent section covering all EVT subtopics. For specific EVT questions, use qualifiers: bridging IVT+EVT → 4.7.1; anterior circulation EVT eligibility (time windows 0-6h and 6-24h, ASPECTS thresholds, ICA/M1 occlusion, large ischemic core) → 4.7.2; posterior/basilar EVT → 4.7.3; EVT techniques (stent retriever vs aspiration, anesthesia, balloon guide catheter, medium/distal vessel occlusion, tandem occlusion) → 4.7.4; pediatric EVT → 4.7.5. Route general EVT eligibility questions to 4.7.2 as that has the most recommendations (8).

subtopics.addresses: Concomitant With IVT — Bridging Therapy (§4.7.1). The IVT + EVT combination question: should IVT be given before EVT if the patient is eligible for both, or should EVT be performed alone ('direct-to-thrombectomy')? Covers the bridging trials (DIRECT-MT, DEVT, SKIP, MR CLEAN NO IV, SWIFT DIRECT, DIRECT-SAFE, MR CLEAN-LATE) and the conclusion that IVT before EVT is recommended when both are indicated and no contraindications exist. Does NOT cover standalone EVT eligibility criteria (see §4.7.2). Does NOT cover standalone IVT eligibility (see §4.6.1).

subtopics.qualifier: adult patients (time windows, ASPECTS, vessels, large core)
subtopics.addresses: Endovascular Thrombectomy for Adult Patients (§4.7.2). The main EVT eligibility section — 8 recommendations, the largest EVT subsection. Covers: 0-6h standard window (ASPECTS ≥6, ICA or M1 occlusion, NIHSS ≥6, pre-stroke mRS 0-1); 6-24h extended window with imaging selection (DAWN, DEFUSE-3); large ischemic core EVT (SELECT2, ANGEL-ASPECT, RESCUE-Japan LIMIT — benefit with ASPECTS 3-5 / core volume 50-100 mL); medium and distal vessel occlusions (M2 nondominant, M3, A2, P2 — EVT not recommended, COR 3 No Benefit); anterior circulation only. Route 'Is this patient an EVT candidate?' / 'EVT eligibility' / 'ASPECTS threshold' questions here. Does NOT cover posterior circulation / basilar EVT (see §4.7.3). Does NOT cover technique selection (see §4.7.4). Does NOT cover pediatric EVT (see §4.7.5).

Posterior Circulation Stroke — EVT (§4.7.3). EVT for basilar artery occlusion and vertebral artery occlusion. Covers the posterior circulation trials (ATTENTION, BAOCHE, BASICS, BEST) and the conclusion that EVT is reasonable for basilar occlusion within 24h in patients with moderate-to-severe deficit (NIHSS ≥10 in some trials) and PC-ASPECTS ≥6. Also addresses the NIHSS 6-9 / mild posterior stroke case where effectiveness is not well established. Route 'basilar EVT', 'basilar artery occlusion', 'posterior circulation thrombectomy', 'PC-ASPECTS' questions here. Does NOT cover anterior circulation EVT (see §4.7.2).

Endovascular Techniques (§4.7.4). HOW the EVT procedure is performed, not whether it should be performed: stent retriever vs direct aspiration (ASTER, COMPASS, ASTER2 — both reasonable first-line), general anesthesia vs procedural sedation (GOLIATH, AnStroke, SIESTA — both acceptable), balloon guide catheter use (effectiveness uncertain), and EVT for medium/distal vessel occlusions (M2, M3, A2 — COR 3 No Benefit). Route 'stent retriever vs aspiration', 'EVT anesthesia', 'ADAPT technique', 'balloon guide catheter' questions here. Does NOT cover WHO is an EVT candidate (see §4.7.2).

Endovascular Thrombectomy in Pediatric Patients (§4.7.5). EVT in patients <18 years old. Covers the limited evidence base in children, case-series data, and the recommendation that EVT may be reasonable in selected pediatric patients with LVO when performed at experienced centers. Does NOT cover adult EVT criteria (see §4.7.2). Does NOT cover pediatric IVT (see IVT special circumstances §4.6.5).

topics.addresses: Early antiplatelet therapy after AIS: aspirin within 48 hours of onset (recommended); antiplatelet timing after IVT (risk in first 24h not well established); glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors (tirofiban efficacy uncertain, abciximab NOT recommended); DAPT (dual antiplatelet) for noncardioembolic minor stroke/TIA — aspirin + clopidogrel for 21-90 days (COR 1), aspirin + ticagrelor for 30 days (COR 2a); single antiplatelet (aspirin, clopidogrel, or aspirin-dipyridamole) for long-term prevention; IV aspirin within 90 min of IVT is harmful. 18 recommendations — the second-largest section. Route here for questions about aspirin, clopidogrel, ticagrelor, DAPT, antiplatelet timing, or antiplatelet after thrombolysis.

Acute anticoagulation in the first days after AIS: early oral anticoagulation for AF (selected milder patients, DOACs started days 3-5); anticoagulation for carotid stenosis or dissection (uncertain benefit); anticoagulation after hemorrhagic transformation (may be considered case-by-case); argatroban NOT effective as IVT adjunct; early parenteral anticoagulation within 48h does NOT reduce recurrence or improve outcomes and increases bleeding. 6 recommendations. Route here for questions about heparin, LMWH, DOACs, warfarin, anticoagulation timing, or AF management after stroke. NOT for long-term secondary prevention anticoagulation.

Treatments to increase cerebral blood flow through volume or pressure manipulation: hemodilution, high-dose albumin, chemical vasodilators (pentoxifylline), and mechanical devices (counterpulsation, sphenopalatine ganglion stimulation). ALL are NOT recommended — both recommendations are COR 3 (No Benefit/Harm). 2 recommendations. Very narrow topic, both negative. Route here only for specific questions about these interventions.

Pharmacological and nonpharmacological neuroprotective agents: NO neuroprotective treatment is currently recommended (COR 3: No Benefit). Covers agents tested and found not effective: nerinetide (NA-1, PSD-95 inhibitor, ESCAPE-NA1 trial), uric acid, magnesium, hypothermia as neuroprotection. 1 recommendation (negative). IMPORTANT: Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) is NOT a neuroprotective agent — HBO for air embolism is covered under Airway and Oxygenation (4.1). Remote ischemic conditioning (RIC) as prehospital neuroprotection is in Prehospital Assessment (2.3). Route here ONLY for questions about neuroprotective drugs or the concept of neuroprotection in stroke.

Emergency carotid endarterectomy (CEA) and carotid angioplasty/stenting (CAS) for acute stroke or unstable neurological status caused by high-grade carotid stenosis or occlusion, including tandem occlusions. Benefit is uncertain (COR 2b). 1 recommendation. Route here for questions about emergency carotid surgery, carotid stenting in acute stroke, or tandem occlusion management (though tandem occlusion EVT technique is also in 4.7.4).

Organized inpatient stroke unit care: specialized interdisciplinary stroke care units with trained teams are recommended for all AIS patients regardless of age. 1 recommendation. Route here for questions about stroke units vs general wards, stroke unit staffing, or whether a patient should be in a stroke unit.

Swallowing assessment and management after stroke: bedside swallow screening before any oral intake (recommended); screening by speech pathologists or trained professionals; instrumental evaluation (FEES, videofluoroscopy) for patients who fail bedside screening; oral hygiene protocols to reduce pneumonia risk; pharyngeal electrical stimulation (PES) to reduce dysphagia severity and aspiration (COR 2a); PES after tracheotomy/ventilation (uncertain benefit). 6 recommendations. Route here for questions about swallowing, aspiration, dysphagia screening, NPO status, oral intake, PES, or feeding assessment.

Nutritional management after stroke: start enteral diet within 7 days of admission; nutritional screening within 48 hours; nasogastric tube initially for dysphagia patients (switch to PEG only if needed beyond 2-3 weeks). 3 recommendations. Route here for questions about tube feeding, enteral nutrition, NG tubes, PEG placement, or nutritional assessment.

Venous thromboembolism (VTE) prevention in immobile stroke patients: intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) recommended as first-line for immobile patients; subcutaneous heparin (UFH or LMWH) is reasonable but benefit over no prophylaxis is uncertain in patients already on IPC; LMWH vs UFH — no clear benefit of one over the other; elastic compression stockings (GPC) cause HARM (skin breakdown, ulceration) and should NOT be used. 5 recommendations. Route here for questions about DVT prevention, blood clot prevention, compression devices, heparin prophylaxis, or TED stockings after stroke.

Screening and treatment of depression after stroke: structured depression screening is recommended using validated inventories; treatment with antidepressants and/or psychotherapy for diagnosed PSD. 2 recommendations. IMPORTANT: SSRIs for motor recovery are covered in Rehabilitation (5.7), NOT here. This section is specifically about depression diagnosis and treatment.

General medical management issues during stroke hospitalization: palliative care referral for appropriate patients; prophylactic antibiotics NOT beneficial; indwelling urinary catheters should NOT be routine (infection risk). 3 recommendations. Route here for questions about catheter use, infection prevention, antibiotics, or palliative care in stroke patients.

Early rehabilitation after stroke: formal interdisciplinary rehabilitation assessment and provision recommended for all AIS patients; SSRIs are NOT effective for motor recovery or functional skills (COR 3: Harm — this is about motor recovery, NOT depression treatment); very early high-dose mobilization within 24 hours is NOT recommended. 3 recommendations. Route here for questions about physical therapy, occupational therapy, mobilization timing, motor recovery, or SSRIs for recovery. For SSRIs for depression, see Post-Stroke Depression (5.5).

General approach to cerebral edema after large cerebral or cerebellar infarction: early goals-of-care discussions, close neurological monitoring, early transfer to neurosurgical-capable centers for at-risk patients. 3 general recommendations. For specific management, use qualifiers: medical management (osmotic therapy, IV glibenclamide, hypothermia — all not effective or uncertain) → 6.2; supratentorial surgical decompression (hemicraniectomy for malignant MCA infarction, age-based guidance <=60 vs >60) → 6.3; cerebellar infarction surgery (ventriculostomy for obstructive hydrocephalus, suboccipital decompression for volumes >=35mL) → 6.4.

subtopics.addresses: Brain Swelling — Medical Management (§6.2). Non-surgical treatment of cerebral edema after large ischemic stroke. Covers: osmotic therapy (mannitol, hypertonic saline — reasonable bridge based on observational data); IV glibenclamide (CHARM trial — no benefit); prophylactic/therapeutic hypothermia (no benefit). Does NOT cover surgical decompression (see §6.3 for supratentorial hemicraniectomy, §6.4 for cerebellar decompression). Does NOT cover ICH management or traumatic edema.

Supratentorial Infarction — Surgical Management (§6.3). Decompressive hemicraniectomy for malignant MCA infarction. Covers: age-based recommendations (strong benefit ≤60y based on DECIMAL, DESTINY, HAMLET pooled analysis; reasonable but less certain >60y based on DESTINY-II); timing (within 48h of symptom onset); patient selection (large MCA territory infarct, deteriorating level of consciousness). Does NOT cover cerebellar infarction surgery (see §6.4). Does NOT cover medical management alone (see §6.2).

Cerebellar Infarction — Surgical Management (§6.4). Posterior fossa surgery for large cerebellar stroke. Covers: ventriculostomy for obstructive hydrocephalus, suboccipital decompressive craniectomy (recommended for cerebellar infarct volume typically ≥35 mL or clinical deterioration from mass effect on the brainstem), and timing considerations. Does NOT cover supratentorial hemicraniectomy (see §6.3). Does NOT cover medical management (see §6.2).

topics.addresses: Post-stroke seizure management: antiseizure medication is recommended for unprovoked seizures after AIS; prophylactic antiseizure medication is NOT recommended to prevent seizures or improve outcomes. 2 recommendations. Route here for questions about seizures, anticonvulsants, epilepsy, or seizure prophylaxis after stroke.

Post-IVT and post-EVT monitoring and complication management — monitoring protocol (Table 7), bleeding after IVT (Table 5), angioedema after IVT (Table 6), BP targets after thrombolysis/thrombectomy, stroke unit admission, neurological monitoring, discharge timing. NOTE: There is no single guideline section for post-treatment management; this content is assembled from multiple sections.

subtopics.addresses: Post-IVT monitoring (Table 7 + §4.6.1 adverse-event recs). Monitoring protocol after IV thrombolysis — neurological checks, vital signs, BP thresholds, and adverse-event surveillance for sICH (Table 5) and angioedema (Table 6). Does NOT cover the drug-choice decision between alteplase and tenecteplase (see IVT §4.6.2) — that is pre-IVT, not post.

Post-EVT blood pressure management (§4.3). Covers BP targets after successful recanalization (lower targets typically SBP <160 or <140 based on BP-TARGET, OPTIMAL-BP, ENCHANTED-2/MT). Also covers BP targets after failed recanalization. Does NOT cover general AIS BP targets without reperfusion therapy (those are the other recs in §4.3). Does NOT cover neurological monitoring protocol per se.

Bleeding after IVT (Table 5). Management algorithm for symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage (sICH) after IV thrombolysis: stop alteplase infusion, cryoprecipitate, platelets, tranexamic acid/aminocaproic acid, reversal agents, ICU-level monitoring, neurosurgical consultation. Defines sICH (ECASS, NINDS, SITS-MOST criteria). Does NOT cover how to DECIDE whether IVT was appropriate (see §4.6.1). Does NOT cover hemorrhagic transformation without IVT exposure.

Angioedema after IVT (Table 6). Management algorithm for orolingual angioedema as an alteplase complication — occurs in ~1-5% of patients, higher with concurrent ACE-inhibitor use. Covers airway protection, antihistamine (H1 + H2 blockade), corticosteroids, epinephrine, icatibant, and when to call anesthesia for airway management. Does NOT cover sICH (see Table 5).

topics.addresses: IVT eligibility decision-making: who should and should not receive IV thrombolysis. Covers: disabling vs non-disabling deficit assessment (Table 4); contraindications and safety checks (Table 8 checklist); faster treatment improves outcomes regardless of NIHSS; IVT within 4.5h of onset; pre-IVT blood glucose check required; hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia management before IVT; patient already on antiplatelet therapy (IVT still recommended); IV aspirin within 90 min of IVT is harmful; treating physician preparation for sICH, angioedema, and other adverse effects. 14 recommendations — the largest IVT subsection. Route here for 'Can I give tPA to this patient?' questions.

subtopics.addresses: IVT indications (Table 4). The table listing patient situations where IV thrombolysis is considered indicated vs uncertain vs not indicated, organized by time window (0-3h, 3-4.5h, extended) and deficit severity. Also addresses the disabling vs non-disabling deficit assessment for minor stroke. Does NOT cover contraindications (see qualifier 'contraindications' → Table 8). Does NOT cover drug choice (see IVT §4.6.2).

IVT contraindications (Table 8). The authoritative contraindication checklist, organized by tier: Absolute contraindications (e.g. CT with extensive hypodensity, active intracranial hemorrhage, recent neurosurgery <14d, aortic dissection), Relative contraindications (e.g. recent major surgery, GI bleeding, seizure at onset), and Benefit-May-Exceed-Risk situations. This is what clinicians reach for when asking 'is IVT safe in this patient?'. Route all 'is X a contraindication to tPA' questions here. Does NOT cover the decision framework (see qualifier 'indications' → Table 4). Does NOT cover management of adverse events after IVT has already been given (see Tables 5 and 6).

Disabling deficit assessment (Table 4). Defines what counts as a disabling deficit when deciding whether to give IVT for minor stroke. Disabling examples: complete hemianopia, severe aphasia, severe hemi-attention, weakness limiting sustained effort against gravity. NOT disabling: isolated mild aphasia, isolated facial droop, mild cortical hand weakness, mild hemisensory loss, mild hemiataxia. Emphasises individual assessment and the principle that NIHSS alone does not suffice (e.g. isolated leg weakness can be NIHSS 2 but disabling). Route 'what are disabling deficits' / 'minor stroke IVT' questions here.

Non-disabling deficit assessment (Table 4). Companion to the 'disabling' qualifier: lists deficits that are generally NOT considered disabling and where IVT is not routinely recommended (PRISMS trial, MaRISS registry). Same table as the disabling qualifier, different framing. Route 'mild stroke — should I treat?' questions where the deficit is clearly non-disabling here.

Symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage (sICH) management after IVT (Table 5). Same content as the Post-Treatment Management → 'bleeding after IVT' qualifier — duplicated here because clinicians asking about IVT safety often follow up with 'what if there's a bleed?'. Covers sICH definitions (ECASS, NINDS, SITS-MOST), stopping alteplase, cryoprecipitate, platelets, tranexamic acid, neurosurgery consult.

Orolingual angioedema after IVT (Table 6). Same content as Post-Treatment Management → 'angioedema after IVT'. Included here because angioedema is part of the pre-IVT risk conversation when the patient is on an ACE-inhibitor.

IVT dosing and administration (Table 7). Alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) as 10% bolus + 90% infusion over 60 minutes. Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg) as single IV bolus. Includes monitoring protocol during and after infusion: vital signs and neurological checks every 15 min for 2h, every 30 min for 6h, every 60 min for 24h, no antiplatelet/anticoagulant for 24h, follow-up imaging at 24h. Route 'tPA dose', 'alteplase dose', 'tenecteplase dose', 'IVT monitoring' questions here. Does NOT cover drug SELECTION (see IVT §4.6.2).

topics.addresses: The full EVT eligibility decision tree by age group (≤28 days, 28 d–5 y, 6–17 y, ≥18 y), vessel occlusion (LVO anterior, MVO dominant/non-dominant M2, DVO, basilar), time from onset (0–6 h, 6–24 h, >24 h), tissue injury (ASPECTS, PC ASPECTS, salvageable brain tissue), and premorbid mRS. Maps each combination to a Class of Recommendation (COR 1, 2a, 2b, 3: No Benefit, or IDD). Route here for questions like 'who gets EVT', 'EVT in pediatric patients', 'EVT for distal vessel occlusion', 'basilar thrombectomy eligibility', 'EVT with low ASPECTS', 'EVT with premorbid disability'.

topics.topic: DAPT for Minor Stroke and TIA
topics.addresses: The antiplatelet selection algorithm (SAPT vs DAPT) for acute minor noncardioembolic ischemic stroke and TIA. Branches on revascularization eligibility, LKN window (<24 h, 24–72 h), presence of atherosclerotic disease (>50%), NIHSS severity (≤3, 4–5, ≤5), ABCD2 score (≥4), and CYP2C19 loss-of-function allele status. Covers DAPT regimens from the CHANCE, CHANCE 2, POINT, THALES, and INSPIRES trials. Route here for questions like 'DAPT for minor stroke', 'antiplatelet after TIA', 'clopidogrel + aspirin duration', 'CYP2C19 testing for antiplatelet', 'ticagrelor in stroke'.
